item.title: A whole lot of nothing
item.content:encoded: This is an interesting idea, but I feel like it drags on too long. A large portion of the article is dedicated to redundantly establishing that the dog is indestructible and a menace, and… that's it. There isn't any payoff. It isn't sad or scary or funny or thought provoking, it's just there. A lot. To me, it feels like a wasted opportunity. The concept of "indestructible, troublesome dog" can make for some interesting stories (think Lilo and Stitch), and maybe there are/will be some fun tales with this scip. But judging the article strictly by its own merit, I have to downvote. Sorry.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Arial is under All Rights Reserved and is not compliant with CC 3.0. Image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really needs a test like:

Stressor: SCP-3042-1 instructed to continuously discipline SCP-3042 while verbalizing "bad dog!" at it.
Result: For the duration of discipline SCP-3042 reacted in a manner consistent with expected canine behavior; whimpering and adopting submissive postures. As soon as discipline ceased SCP-3042 returned to its typical behavior with no evidence of lasting modification. SCP-3042 reported that the discipline routine was "exhausting" and "didn't make it any less annoying".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can we have a tale idea of one SCP-3042-1 being a dog trainer? It should be rather interesting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-3042 vs SCP-682?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally, an anomalous dog I DON'T want to adopt. +1

I was curious about the inclusion of SCP-484 but I think your reasoning for it makes perfect sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This creature would probably survive going from one end of 682 to another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll be honest, I upvoted this solely because this dog looks so cute. Even though it'd be a great pain to have it follow you everywhere to the ends of the Earth, ha ha ha. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The testing log seems unnecessary. The point of it being indestructible has been made and doesn't need to be hammered in that much.

Oh and feed this to 682

item.title: Funny Story regarding this SCP.
item.content:encoded:
So, I got bored and googled SCP on Wattpad.
I found a SCP-049 [labelled as SCP-49 on the fanfiction xD] where the reader was an SCP. The reader was labelled as SCP-3042. This is quite the plottwist!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: That's great, thank you!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Done.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

EDIT: As no source was provided within the time limit, the images have been removed and the author notified. They are welcome to add them back if a complient source can be found.

EDIT: Image sources are good.

item.title: Big thanks!
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for what was clearly a close and careful read; I appreciate you taking the time. I have no excuse but lazy brained foolishness, but I was smart enough to fix it all :)

Okay. About SCP-484s inclusion. To assist the story, I needed a way to quietly get this thing into Foundation hands; I decided against them just running after a 4-legged natural disaster. It had to have an owner who may regularly kill people and I thought drug lord. The Foundation then required a reason to care. I chose a drug SCP that could plausibly be physically recreated and also I just thought was pretty cool. The inclusion is meant to be the impetus for the Foundation taking action. I can't control if my head-canon makes you more accepting of the inclusion, but I do hope to display that it was a considered inclusion. If I can take you down from Belgian waffle to Eggo waffle though I'll take that too. 😁

Addendum: I always thought 'thus far' was one word and I learned something today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I love this article (+1), it is in serious need of a spell-check:

In the unlikely event of SCP-3042 and SCP-3042-1 becoming separated outside containment house gounds, swift response specialists will expediently deliver SCP-3042-1 to SCP-3042. During test periods, delivery of subjects to test site will be executed by no fewer than five (5) members of the Specal Fugitive Transportation unit.

Further medical testing has proved thusfar imposible due to the anomalous traits of SCP-3042.

Thusfar no stimulus tested has distracted SCP-3042 from its focus on SCP-3042-1.

Request for termination was granted, consdered a "research opportunity" by site director.

In its travels, SCP-3042 dug through two steel reinforced cobcrete walls,

The footnotes are also pointless, especially the bit about SCP-484, and don't add anything to your SCP and should be deleted as waffle.

Fix these, and it'll be great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dear god.
It's like Pepe Le Pew but faster and less uncomfortable.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I understand your desire to see a little suspense or extended tension, but I believe that change is far too fundamental for me to comfortably execute with this particular entry. That said, you have reminded me that I have not dipped into a ticking clock/survival horror-esque feel in any of my submissions; I'll keep you in mind for another project. I do appreciate your input.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The invincibility is fine, but the extreme strength it has seems a bit much. The scary part is that you can't escape from it. So it has to eventually break any barrier, but it's not really necessary for it to smash through everything in two seconds flat.

In fact it's probably better if you can get a brief respite from the thing. That opens up a lot more narrative space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What you see now is post-edit. I received previous feedback saying the tone sounded unprofessional so I made it more clinical.
Can you please explain in more detail what you're seeing? I'm not certain what is troubling you about the example you gave. Please lend me your eyes.

UPDATE: You got me thinking about some of what I had going on in there. I think some brevity was in order. I cleaned up some.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's got a few awkward​ sounding sentences with "SCP-3042 seems in an extreme state of health" being the most egregious I remember. I echo the comments on this feeling insubstantial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are definitely picking up what I'm putting down here. I'm overjoyed that you've embraced the vision!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for your support! Very happy you like this. I'm not sure I'm ready to change the designation, but I do get what you're saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is subtly terrifying. If it ever went Bad Dog on anyone, it'd be as deadly a SCP as 682 or Able.

The image of it chewing through graphene - there's a vivid demonstration of its anomalous quality. The detail of it wearing out the humans it bonds to was also effective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I appreciate you doing so. I'll leave the tagging to the experts from now on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've taken a stab at cleaning the tags for the article. "Dog" and "invincible" are not valid tags (I've changed them to "canine" and "indestructible" which are), and I've added "scp."

In general, it's best not to tag an article at all and let the Tag Team get around to doing it for you so that all tags remain valid. Someone looking for other "dog" articles won't find anything, and someone starting from any one of the other canine articles won't find yours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I'd make the argument for upgrading this to Keter status; even though it's not immediately dangerous, it does leave a detrimental state particularly towards Foundation personnel, and the fact that it can only be contained using- well- victims… kind of makes the case in and of itself.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry to hear this didn't engage you. I will fix the error you found; if you have any more specifics on improvements I will gladly consider them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is full of spelling (eg "veterenary") and capitalisation errors, and the clinical tone is weak. Also, it doesn't do anything surprising with the "indestructible loving pet" premise - I'm not surprised to hear that this is a DnD character transposed into Foundation world, as it doesn't have any narrative drive or interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jezuit, this is pretty unhelpful feedback.

While I'm sure the author is glad to hear you enjoyed the skip, you don't actually provide any examples of writing you feel could be more fluid, and generally allude to "edits will fix it" without providing any context.

Keep in mind, "I like it!" is perfectly valid crit, but citing problems and not giving examples or guidance is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your sentiments were echoed. I trimmed a bit here and there. I'm very glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Images cited. Thanks for the reminder! I'll take the tone note. I committed myself to the logs in the body and didn't reconsider if I actually needed that many; I'll review that. Thanks for your input!

UPDATE: Did I really have that many logs? I surprise myself! I pruned some.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this is a very well thought out skip, with a very original idea. The writing could use a little work to feel more fluid, but I'm sure it's nothing a few edits won't eventually fix. I'm sure some of the more experienced members will be able to be a lot more specific than I. Overall, great job!

Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a decent skip. Your tone is spot on on most places, though in the first part of the description I would recommend referring to it as SCP-3042 rather than dog. In the researcher's note, removing the 'in all cases' on the end would make it a bit smoother to read. In fact, you could probably just say SCP-3042-1 instead of writing out 'the individual SCP-3042 is showing affection to'. Apart from those, your tone is excellent and I haven't noticed any spelling or grammar mistakes. The SCP itself is interesting. Though the 'invincible' thing is hard to pull of, I feel like you've done an acceptable job of it. The testing logs seem a bit redundant and could probably be shortened. All they do is show that it can break through anything and can't be injured, which I feel could be done just as effectively with fewer logs.

Also, what license do the images use? Said license may require 'giving credit', and if so you must link to the image sources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a lot! Happy for your keen eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha! I knew I would miss one somewhere. Thanks, Dr. Emperor!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed two other SCP-XXXX.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture caption still had SCP-XXXX in it. I've taken the liberty of changing it to SCP-3042 for you.

item.title: A new incarnation
item.content:encoded:
The origin of this creature are in a Dungeons and Dragons campaign. After creating this cursed familiar, I wondered how the Foundation would handle it!

Dog Source (wikipedia Commons)
https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Quite_the_happy_dog.jpg
